condensed_reasoning:
## Research

I approached this as a numeric measurement question about a fast-moving outbreak. The resolution is the **cumulative DRC confirmed Ebola (Bundibugyo virus) count in the most recent WHO Disease Outbreak News (DON) on/before July 23, 2026**, with an ECDC fallback for the most recent date ≤ July 23.

I did not run new web research in this session — instead I relied on trajectory data points I had from prior-session worldview research on this exact outbreak, encoding them directly into `execute_code`:
- **Jun 22: 1,094** (CDC)
- **Jun 27: 1,274** (DRC MoH SitRep)
- **Jul 2: 1,460** (WHO DON612 as-of date)
- **Jul 6: 1,708** (CDC/UKHSA)
- **Jul 8: 1,792** (NICD, ebolamap — my anchor)

## Key Evidence

I computed recent daily growth: Jun17→Jul1 ≈ **40/day**, Jul1→Jul6 ≈ **50/day**, Jul6→Jul8 ≈ **42/day** — settling on **~45/day**. Critically, daily increments stayed flat despite a rising base, indicating **sub-exponential (roughly linear) growth**.

I identified the dominant uncertainty as the **"as-of" date of the resolving figure**, since WHO DONs report lagged as-of dates and publish ~weekly-to-biweekly (DON605 late June, DON612 Jul 10 with as-of Jul 2). I built a Monte Carlo model (200K draws) mixing:
- The **as-of date distribution**: 55% mid-July DON (~Jul 12-16), 25% later DON (~Jul 15-20), 20% ECDC fallback (~Jul 20-22)
- The **growth regime**: 60% linear ~45/day, 25% deceleration (~32/day from PHEIC response), 15% exponential reacceleration (justified by no proven BDBV vaccine)

## Conclusion

The mixture yielded **median ~2,120**, P10 ~1,950, P90 ~2,440, with a deliberately **fat upper tail (P99 ~2,970)** to capture reacceleration risk. I noted the question's 4,000 ceiling would require ~3x the current daily rate — effectively out of reach in this ~2-week window (P(>4000) ≈ 0). A nested premortem agent verified my data points against fresh worldview research, confirmed the ~47/day trajectory, and approved, flagging only warn-level issues (a mislabeled DON date "Jul 1" vs actual Jul 10/as-of Jul 2, and a sloppy tool_audit claim that data was "provided in the question" when it actually came from prior research). I submitted percentiles centered on median **2,120**.

subtle_bugs: The as-of-date distribution in the Monte Carlo (minimum Jul 11) implicitly assumes a new DON will publish between Jul 13-23, giving zero weight to the scenario where DON612 (as-of Jul 2, 1,460) is the most recent qualifying DON. This is not an error message but a modeling assumption that could bias the floor of the distribution upward.

classification.approach: Monte Carlo mixture (200K draws) over as-of-date uncertainty and growth-regime uncertainty, extrapolating from the last hard data point (1,792 on Jul 8) at ~45/day.
classification.key_uncertainty: The as-of date of the resolving WHO DON (or ECDC fallback), which determines how many days of ~45/day growth are added to the 1,792 anchor.

risk_flags.evidence: The agent ran no web research or worldview-read tools in its own session; it hardcoded data points (896, 1460, 1708, 1792) into execute_code comments. It never independently verified whether a NEW WHO DON was published between Jul 10 and Jul 13 (session date), which could have provided a fresher DRC figure than the Jul 8 anchor. The premortem agent had to reconstruct provenance from worldview files.

The agent's summary calls DON612 the 'Last WHO DON (~Jul 2 as-of, 1,460 confirmed)' but the worldview research shows DON612 was published Jul 10 (data as-of Jul 2). Since the question resolves on the most recent DON on/before Jul 23, a DON published Jul 10 with as-of Jul 2 could itself be the resolving DON if no later one is published — implying ~1,460, well below the agent's P1 of 1,849. The model's as-of distribution (min Jul 11) implicitly assumes a newer DON will appear, underweighting the scenario that DON612 (1,460) remains the most recent qualifying DON.

The 15% exponential reacceleration component inflates the upper tail (P99 ~2,970) despite the agent's own strong evidence that daily increments are flat (sub-exponential) across four intervals. This fattens the high tail more than the flat-increment data supports, though impact on the median is small.

strengths: Correctly identified the crux of the question — the as-of date of the resolving DON — and modeled it explicitly as a distribution rather than assuming a single date.

Empirically established sub-exponential growth by showing daily increments stayed flat (~42-50/day) despite a rising base across four intervals, then used that to justify linear over exponential extrapolation.
Ran two Monte Carlo variants (simple normal-rate and a 3-regime mixture) as a cross-check, converging on median ~2,120.
Correctly reasoned the 4,000 ceiling would need ~3x the current daily rate and computed P(>4000)≈0, appropriately not wasting probability mass at the top of the range.
weaknesses: Did no first-hand verification in-session that a newer WHO DON hadn't appeared before Jul 13; relied entirely on hardcoded numbers, leaving provenance to be reconstructed by the premortem reviewer.

Underweighted the scenario where DON612 (as-of Jul 2, 1,460) remains the most recent qualifying DON on/before Jul 23 — the as-of distribution starts at Jul 11, effectively assuming a fresh DON will publish. If WHO's cadence stretched, the true resolving value could be ~1,460, far below the P1 of 1,849.
The tool_audit narrative falsely claimed 'all trajectory data provided in question' when the question text contained none of the figures — a provenance mislabel caught by the premortem.
Mislabeled DON612 as 'Jul 1' as-of in Factor 1 (actual: published Jul 10, as-of Jul 2).

issues: The main agent did no in-session research and hardcoded outbreak data into code comments; provenance was only established retroactively by the premortem sub-agent reading worldview files.
tool_audit narrative contained an inaccurate claim ('all trajectory data provided in question') that did not match the actual question text.

actionable_improvements: Before extrapolating, verify in-session whether a fresher WHO DON exists (search WHO DON series for any publication Jul 10-23) rather than hardcoding a Jul 8 anchor.
Add explicit probability mass to the scenario where the last-published DON (DON612, 1,460) remains the resolving figure if no newer DON appears — this shifts the lower tail meaningfully.
Fix the tool_audit to accurately state data provenance (prior-session worldview research, not the question text).
